edges.description: P5CS deficiency and reduced glutamate-derived amino acid availability are part of the multisystem growth-failure phenotype in recessive disease.
edges.source: P5CS deficiency and proline biosynthesis impairment

edges.description: The same multisystem P5CS-deficiency state can manifest clinically as failure to thrive.
edges.source: P5CS deficiency and proline biosynthesis impairment

edges.description: Reduced urea-cycle amino acid availability manifests as hyperammonemia in classic P5CS deficiency.
Hyperammonemia is measured clinically as increased blood ammonia.
Hypoornithinemia is a direct biochemical readout of the urea-cycle amino acid branch.
Hypocitrullinemia is reported with the same low urea-cycle amino acid profile.
Hypoargininemia reports downstream impairment of arginine biosynthesis.
Thin, inelastic connective tissue in the ALDH18A1 cutis laxa spectrum makes superficial veins visibly prominent.
Speech and language delay is part of the broader neurodevelopmental involvement.
Cataracts are a recurrent ocular feature of the ALDH18A1/ARCL3A spectrum, but the precise intermediate lens pathway downstream of the cellular lipid droplet phenotype remains unresolved.
Lower plasma proline reports impaired P5CS-dependent proline biosynthesis.
edges.source: P5CS deficiency and proline biosynthesis impairment

edges.description: Lower plasma ornithine reports the urea-cycle amino acid depletion branch downstream of P5CS deficiency.
Lower plasma citrulline reports downstream urea-cycle amino acid depletion.
Lower plasma arginine reports downstream impairment of the arginine biosynthetic branch.
Elevated ammonia reports impaired nitrogen handling in the severe P5CS deficiency branch.
Lower cellular glutathione reports the antioxidant-metabolism branch in ALDH18A1-deficient cells.
Lower cellular putrescine reports impaired ornithine-derived polyamine biosynthesis in the antioxidant branch.

evidence.reference_title: Compound heterozygous mutations in two different domains of ALDH18A1 do not affect the amino acid levels in a patient with hereditary spastic paraplegia.
evidence.snippet:
Mutations in ALDH18A1 can cause autosomal recessive and dominant hereditary
spastic paraplegia and autosomal recessive and dominant cutis laxa.

evidence.reference_title: Alteration of ornithine metabolism leads to dominant and recessive hereditary spastic paraplegia.
evidence.snippet:
catalyses the first and common step of proline and ornithine biosynthesis from
glutamate.

evidence.reference_title: ALDH18A1 / P5CS deficiency (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: ALDH18A1 | HGNC:9722 | P5CS deficiency | MONDO:0100126 | SD | Definitive

nodes.description: P5CS deficiency leads to reduced glutathione levels and decreased polyamine (putrescine) biosynthesis from ornithine, impairing cellular antioxidant defenses. This oxidative vulnerability may contribute to neuronal degeneration in motor neurons and cortical neurons.

evidence.reference_title: Functional assessment of homozygous ALDH18A1 variants reveals alterations in amino acid and antioxidant metabolism.
evidence.snippet:
identified reduced abundance of glutamate and several metabolites derived from
glutamate, including proline and glutathione. Biosynthesis of the polyamine
putrescine, derived from ornithine, was also decreased in patient fibroblasts,

evidence.reference_title: Functional assessment of homozygous ALDH18A1 variants reveals alterations in amino acid and antioxidant metabolism.
evidence.snippet:
Biosynthesis of the polyamine
putrescine, derived from ornithine, was also decreased in patient fibroblasts,
highlighting the functional consequence on another metabolic pathway involved in
antioxidant responses in the cell.

evidence.reference_title: Hyperammonemia with reduced ornithine, citrulline, arginine and proline: a new inborn error caused by a mutation in the gene encoding delta(1)-pyrroline-5-carboxylate synthase.
evidence.snippet:
Their metabolic phenotype includes hyperammonemia, hypoornithinemia,
hypocitrullinemia, hypoargininemia and hypoprolinemia.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated ammonia reports impaired nitrogen handling in the severe P5CS deficiency branch.

nodes.description: Bilateral cataracts are frequent in both dominant and recessive forms, reported in approximately 60% of ARCL3A patients and in some SPG9A families.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of ALDH18A1 function is associated with a cellular lipid droplet phenotype suggesting a link between autosomal recessive cutis laxa type 3A and Warburg Micro syndrome.
evidence.snippet: bilateral cataracts have been seen in 6/10 cases and so appear more frequent than in patients with PYCR1 mutations

evidence.reference_title: Functional assessment of homozygous ALDH18A1 variants reveals alterations in amino acid and antioxidant metabolism.
evidence.snippet:
identified reduced abundance of glutamate and several metabolites derived from
glutamate, including proline and glutathione.

readouts.interpretation: Lower cellular glutathione reports the antioxidant-metabolism branch in ALDH18A1-deficient cells.

nodes.description: ALDH18A1-mutant fibroblasts show enlarged lipid droplets after oleate loading, a cellular phenotype similar to findings reported in Warburg Micro syndrome models.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of ALDH18A1 function is associated with a cellular lipid droplet phenotype suggesting a link between autosomal recessive cutis laxa type 3A and Warburg Micro syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
Patient fibroblasts exhibit a lipid droplet phenotype similar to
that recently reported in Warburg Micro syndrome, a disorder with similar
features but hitherto unrelated cellular etiology.

evidence.reference_title: Functional assessment of homozygous ALDH18A1 variants reveals alterations in amino acid and antioxidant metabolism.
evidence.snippet:
Biosynthesis of the polyamine
putrescine, derived from ornithine, was also decreased in patient fibroblasts,

nodes.description: Wrinkled, inelastic skin with visible veins is a feature of ARCL3A and some SPG9B patients, reflecting deficient proline-dependent collagen synthesis. The degree of skin involvement is variable.

evidence.reference_title: Cutis laxa, fat pads and retinopathy due to ALDH18A1 mutation and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet:
Features of our patient that
have been described in literature included cutis laxa on hands and feet, visible
veins on thorax and abdomen, joint laxity, failure to thrive, short stature,
microcephaly, and severe developmental and speech delay.

nodes.description: Severe speech and language delay accompanies the global developmental delay in ALDH18A1-related cutis laxa.

evidence.reference_title: Cutis laxa, fat pads and retinopathy due to ALDH18A1 mutation and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet:
cutis laxa on hands and feet, visible
veins on thorax and abdomen, joint laxity, failure to thrive, short stature,
microcephaly, and severe developmental and speech delay.

nodes.description: In the autosomal dominant form, heterozygous missense mutations do not abolish P5CS protein production but are proposed to disrupt P5CS oligomer architecture via a dominant-negative effect, reducing enzymatic activity despite normal protein localization.

evidence.reference_title: P5CS expression study in a new family with ALDH18A1-associated hereditary spastic paraplegia SPG9.
evidence.snippet: leading to the proposal that dominant mutations cause ALDH18A1 pathologies by a dominant negative effect

nodes.description: Impaired P5CS function is associated with extracellular matrix-related transcript changes in patient fibroblasts and with the wrinkled, inelastic skin phenotype of ALDH18A1-related cutis laxa.

evidence.reference_title: Functional assessment of homozygous ALDH18A1 variants reveals alterations in amino acid and antioxidant metabolism.
evidence.snippet:
revealed transcript abundance changes in several metabolic and extracellular
matrix-related genes, adding further insight into pathogenic processes
associated with impaired P5CS function.

evidence.reference_title: Cutis laxa, fat pads and retinopathy due to ALDH18A1 mutation and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet:
Autosomal recessive cutis laxa (ARCL) is a connective tissue disorder
characterized by wrinkled, inelastic skin, frequently associated with a
neurologic involvement and multisystem disease.

nodes.description: Failure to thrive occurs in ARCL3A/de Barsy-spectrum disease alongside short stature and the broader growth-failure phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Cutis laxa, fat pads and retinopathy due to ALDH18A1 mutation and review of the literature.

cutis laxa on hands and feet, visible
veins on thorax and abdomen, joint laxity, failure to thrive, short stature,
microcephaly, and severe developmental and speech delay.

nodes.description: Developmental delay is prominent in the recessive form, with variable severity.

evidence.reference_title: Functional assessment of homozygous ALDH18A1 variants reveals alterations in amino acid and antioxidant metabolism.
evidence.snippet: developmental delay, neurological deficits and loose skin.

nodes.description: Short stature and failure to thrive are prominent in SPG9B and ARCL3A, reflecting impaired proline-dependent growth processes.

evidence.reference_title: Cutis laxa, fat pads and retinopathy due to ALDH18A1 mutation and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet:
Features of our patient that
have been described in literature included cutis laxa on hands and feet, visible
veins on thorax and abdomen, joint laxity, failure to thrive, short stature,
microcephaly, and severe developmental and speech delay.

nodes.description: Hyperammonemia was part of the original biallelic P5CS deficiency metabolic phenotype together with reduced ornithine, citrulline, arginine, and proline. It should be interpreted as a reported branch of the severe neurocutaneous spectrum rather than a universal feature of every ALDH18A1 presentation.

evidence.reference_title: Hyperammonemia with reduced ornithine, citrulline, arginine and proline: a new inborn error caused by a mutation in the gene encoding delta(1)-pyrroline-5-carboxylate synthase.
evidence.snippet:
Their metabolic phenotype includes hyperammonemia, hypoornithinemia,
hypocitrullinemia, hypoargininemia and hypoprolinemia.

nodes.description: Cognitive impairment ranging from mild to severe intellectual disability, more prominent in biallelic SPG9B and ARCL3A than in dominant SPG9A.

evidence.reference_title: P5CS expression study in a new family with ALDH18A1-associated hereditary spastic paraplegia SPG9.
evidence.snippet:
conclude that both mutations are disease-causing, that SPG9B associates with
partial P5CS deficiency and that it is clinically more severe than SPG9A, as
reflected in onset age, disability, cognitive status, growth, and dysmorphic
traits.

nodes.description: Joint laxity or hypermobility is part of the connective-tissue phenotype in ALDH18A1-related ARCL3A/de Barsy-spectrum disease, occurring with cutis laxa, visible veins, growth failure, microcephaly, and developmental delay.

evidence.reference_title: Cutis laxa, fat pads and retinopathy due to ALDH18A1 mutation and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet:
Features of our patient that
have been described in literature included cutis laxa on hands and feet, visible
veins on thorax and abdomen, joint laxity, failure to thrive, short stature,
microcephaly, and severe developmental and speech delay.

nodes.description: Microcephaly is reported in approximately 70% of ARCL3A patients and reflects impaired brain growth.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of ALDH18A1 function is associated with a cellular lipid droplet phenotype suggesting a link between autosomal recessive cutis laxa type 3A and Warburg Micro syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Microcephaly has been reported in 7/10 cases, a similar proportion to that seen in patients with PYCR1 mutations.

nodes.description: ALDH18A1-related P5CS deficiency produces a neurological disease spectrum spanning hereditary spastic paraplegia, cognitive impairment, developmental delay, and microcephaly, with recessive SPG9B generally more severe than dominant SPG9A.

evidence.reference_title: Alteration of ornithine metabolism leads to dominant and recessive hereditary spastic paraplegia.
evidence.snippet:
Hereditary spastic paraplegias are heterogeneous neurological disorders
characterized by a pyramidal syndrome with symptoms predominantly affecting the
lower limbs.

evidence.reference_title: Functional assessment of homozygous ALDH18A1 variants reveals alterations in amino acid and antioxidant metabolism.
evidence.snippet: developmental delay, neurological deficits and loose skin.

nodes.description: Loss-of-function mutations in ALDH18A1 reduce P5CS enzymatic activity and impair de novo biosynthesis of proline and ornithine from glutamate. Human plasma amino-acid studies and patient-cell metabolomics show reduced glutamate-derived metabolites, providing the metabolic bridge to downstream antioxidant, extracellular-matrix, and neurodevelopmental abnormalities.
nodes.id: P5CS deficiency and proline biosynthesis impairment

evidence.reference_title: Hyperammonemia with reduced ornithine, citrulline, arginine and proline: a new inborn error caused by a mutation in the gene encoding delta(1)-pyrroline-5-carboxylate synthase.
evidence.snippet:
delta(1)-pyrroline-5-carboxylate synthase (P5CS), a bifunctional ATP- and
NADPH-dependent mitochondrial enzyme, catalyzes the reduction of glutamate to
delta(1)-pyrroline-5-carboxylate, a critical step in the biosynthesis of
proline, ornithine and arginine.

evidence.reference_title: Alteration of ornithine metabolism leads to dominant and recessive hereditary spastic paraplegia.
evidence.snippet:
as well as in two sporadic patients. Low levels of plasma ornithine, citrulline,
arginine and proline in four individuals from two families suggested P5CS
deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Functional assessment of homozygous ALDH18A1 variants reveals alterations in amino acid and antioxidant metabolism.
evidence.snippet:
identified reduced abundance of glutamate and several metabolites derived from
glutamate, including proline and glutathione.

nodes.description: Biallelic ALDH18A1 variants can reduce P5CS oligomer incorporation, protein stability, or both, producing a partial loss-of-function state in patient cells.

nodes.description: Progressive spasticity of the lower limbs is the hallmark feature, presenting as pure hereditary spastic paraplegia in some dominant families and complex HSP with additional neurological features in others.

evidence.reference_title: Alteration of ornithine metabolism leads to dominant and recessive hereditary spastic paraplegia.
evidence.snippet:
identified monoallelic ALDH18A1 mutations segregating in three independent
families with autosomal dominant pure or complex hereditary spastic paraplegia,

nodes.description: Biallelic mutations cause complex spastic paraplegia with marked cognitive impairment, earlier onset, and greater disability than the dominant form.

evidence.reference_title: Alteration of ornithine metabolism leads to dominant and recessive hereditary spastic paraplegia.
evidence.snippet:
families with autosomal recessive transmission of ALDH18A1 mutations, and
predominant complex hereditary spastic paraplegia with marked cognitive
impairment, without any cutaneous abnormality.

nodes.description: P5CS deficiency lowers ornithine, citrulline, and arginine availability, linking the blocked glutamate-derived amino-acid pathway to impaired nitrogen handling. Classic P5CS deficiency can therefore manifest downstream as hyperammonemia together with hypoornithinemia, hypocitrullinemia, hypoargininemia, and hypoprolinemia.
